R

Data Science

Programming

Machine Learning

Data Visualization

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why I’m Learning R
I committed to a 30-day R programming challenge. One script per day. No skipping. No copy-paste-and-forget. Every day I build something small but real, and I write about what I learned so the knowledge actually sticks.
Day 1’s mission was deceptively simple: build a Personal Bio Calculator. Give it your name, birth year, height, and weight — and it tells you your age, your BMI, your BMI category, and a friendly personalized message.
It sounds like a toy. It isn’t. In under 40 lines of code, this project forces you to touch almost every foundational concept in R:

• Variables and assignment (<-)

• Data types (numeric, character, logical, integer)

• Arithmetic operators and precedence

• Built-in functions like Sys.Date(), format(), round(), and as.integer()

• Type coercion

• Conditional logic with if, else if, and else

• String concatenation with paste0()

• Clean console output with cat()
Let’s build it together.

1. Setting Up R and RStudio
If you’ve never written R before, you need two things:

• R — the language itself: https://cran.r-project.org/

• RStudio — the IDE most people actually use: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/
Install both with defaults, open RStudio, and you’ll see four main panels:

• Source (Top-Left): Where you write scripts and save your files.

• Console (Bottom-Left): Where the code actually runs and displays text results.

• Environment (Top-Right): Where your active variables and data objects live.

• Files / Plots / Help (Bottom-Right): Where you can view project directories, generated charts, and documentation.
Create a new project (File → New Project → New Directory) called r-30days, add a folder named day-01, and create a new R script called bio_calculator.R.
💡 Pro Tip: To run a single line of code, press Ctrl + Enter (Windows/Linux) or Cmd + Enter (Mac). To execute the entire script from top to bottom, press Ctrl + Shift + S (or Cmd + Shift + S).

2. The Concepts, In Plain English
Variables
In R, you store values using the specific arrow assignment operator <-:
name <- "Umesh"
age <- 24
is_student <- TRUERead <- as "gets". For example, name gets the value "Umesh". R is strictly case-sensitive, meaning Age and age are treated as two completely distinct variables.
Data Types
TypeExampleCheck withnumeric1.75, 42is.numeric(x)character"hello"is.character(x)logicalTRUE, FALSEis.logical(x)integer10L (the 'L' forces integer mode)is.integer(x)
Arithmetic Operators
5 + 2 # 7 (Addition)
5 / 2 # 2.5 (Division)
5 %% 2 # 1 (Remainder / Modulo)
5 %/% 2 # 2 (Integer Division)
5 ^ 2 # 25 (Exponentiation / Power)Printing and String Manipulation

• print(x) — Quick for debugging, but includes structural quotes around string characters.

• cat(...) — Generates clean, human-readable terminal output and correctly processes \n line breaks. Use this for reports.

• paste0(...) — Glues multiple text strings and variables together without adding spaces between them.

3. The Math: BMI
Body Mass Index is calculated using the following formula:
We will use the official World Health Organization (WHO) weight classification boundaries:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

BMI RangeCategoryLess than 18.5Underweight18.5–24.9Normal weight25.0–29.9Overweight30.0 or HigherObese
To keep the application practical, our script takes the initial height input in centimeters (since that is how most people know it) and handles the conversion to meters automatically behind the scenes.

4. Building the Script, Step by Step
Step 1 — Store the inputs
name <- "Umesh"
birth_year <- 2001
height_cm <- 175
weight_kg <- 68Step 2 — Get the current year automatically
current_year <- as.integer(format(Sys.Date(), "%Y"))
• Sys.Date() returns today's full calendar system date.

• format(..., "%Y") isolates and extracts the 4-digit year as text (e.g., "2026").

• as.integer(...) explicitly shifts that character string into a pure number. This is your first encounter with type coercion, a core pillar of managing data flows in R.
Step 3 — Compute age
age <- current_year - birth_yearStep 4 — Convert height and compute BMI
height_m <- height_cm / 100
bmi <- weight_kg / (height_m ^ 2)
bmi <- round(bmi, 1)Parentheses matter significantly when managing execution order. Always be explicit with mathematical expressions.
Step 5 — Classify the BMI
if (bmi < 18.5) {
category <- "Underweight"
} else if (bmi < 25) {
category <- "Normal weight"
} else if (bmi < 30) {
category <- "Overweight"
} else {
category <- "Obese"
}Step 6 — Build the message
message <- paste0(
"Hi ", name, "!\n",
"You are ", age, " years old.\n",
"Your BMI is ", bmi, " (", category, ").\n",
"Keep learning R every day 🚀"
)Step 7 — Print the report
cat("----- Bio Report -----\n")
cat(message, "\n")
cat("----------------------\n")
5. The Complete Script
Here is the fully unified, syntax-corrected script. It resolves the common line-break errors that happen when copy-pasting nested conditional blocks directly into RStudio:
# ==========================================================
# Personal Bio Calculator — Day 1 of R 30-day plan
# Author: Umesh
# ==========================================================# ---- 1. Inputs -------------------------------------------
name <- "Umesh"
birth_year <- 2001
height_cm <- 175
weight_kg <- 68# ---- 2. Derived values -----------------------------------
current_year <- as.integer(format(Sys.Date(), "%Y"))
age <- current_year - birth_yearheight_m <- height_cm / 100
bmi <- round(weight_kg / (height_m ^ 2), 1)# ---- 3. BMI category -------------------------------------
if (bmi < 18.5) {
category <- "Underweight"
} else if (bmi < 25) {
category <- "Normal weight"
} else if (bmi < 30) {
category <- "Overweight"
} else {
category <- "Obese"
}# ---- 4. Build message ------------------------------------
message <- paste0(
"Hi ", name, "!\n",
"You are ", age, " years old.\n",
"Your height is ", height_cm, " cm and weight is ", weight_kg, " kg.\n",
"Your BMI is ", bmi, " (", category, ").\n",
"Keep learning R every day 🚀"
)# ---- 5. Print report -------------------------------------
cat("----- Bio Report -----\n")
cat(message, "\n")
cat("----------------------\n")Expected Console Output:
----- Bio Report -----
Hi Umesh!
You are 25 years old.
Your height is 175 cm and weight is 68 kg.
Your BMI is 22.2 (Normal weight).
Keep learning R every day 🚀
----------------------
6. Bugs I Hit (So You Don’t Have To)

• object 'height_cm' not found → Occurs if you try running a downstream mathematical formula line before execution has hit and stored the upstream baseline input variables. Fix: Source the entire file completely using Ctrl + Shift + S.

• non-numeric argument to binary operator → Occurs when trying to run standard math arithmetic on character data types. Wrap character string numbers in as.numeric() or as.integer() to cast them correctly first.

• BMI is 22000 instead of 22 → Occurs when calculating using centimeters directly instead of converting to meters. Remember to divide your raw height_cm input by 100 before applying the exponent.

• Output shows \n literally → Occurs if you print raw structured paragraphs via print(). Swap over to using cat() to interpret control commands correctly.

7. What I Actually Learned Today

• R’s assignment operator is <-, not =. While = functions under limited scopes, the traditional arrow syntax is the community idiom for structural global execution.

• Type coercion is a first-class concept. Data variables moving across functions often transform implicitly. Learning how to control those boundaries using as.integer() saves massive debugging hours.

• Sys.Date() and format() open the gateway to R's native date system. Parsing specific components out of internal objects is direct and clean once you master format wildcards like "%Y".

• cat() completely beats print() for human-readable console generation. It cleans up escaping symbols and produces production-grade terminal blocks.

• The Environment panel is your best debugger. Checking live states and current scalar tracking within RStudio balances execution errors immediately.

8. What’s Next
Day 2: Vectors — Marks Analyzer. We will step beyond individual scalar variables to study lists of continuous numeric sequences. We will take a raw array of exam marks and transform it into a robust, automated statistical analysis dashboard using R’s most critical data structure.
The full 30-day plan roadmap, source repositories, and daily logs live out on my GitHub profile. Star the repository if you want to follow along with the daily tracking updates.
See you tomorrow!​
